posts.name: Ian Fitzsimmons

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: Ian, just curious. Do you like Pavelot more than Chandon de Briailles? Just interested in how you compare the two, objectively or subjectively.

posts.name: Ian Fitzsimmons
posts.cooked:
Well, I’m not sure I can give you a straight answer. The styles are so different: CdB generally higher-toned and more elegant, Pavelot showing more guts, imho. Most of what I have is still fairly young, 2005 and later, and I’m not sure I fully appreciate what either producer has to offer yet. I guess I’d also echo Lynch, who, discussing ‘best’ wines, wrote ‘best for what?’

Subjectively, you obviously want to consider mood, food, and company when deciding which to open on a particular occasion. Objectively, Pernand’s Ile de Vergelesses vineyard may have better potential than any of the Pavelot SlBs, even Dominode. The 09 CdB Ile I tasted at one of Maureen’s events a few years ago made a really powerful impression. I would love to sample the SlB Liards from both producers, side by side. I probably have more Pavelot just because I started buying from this domaine sooner than from CdB.

Sorry to meander so much.

posts.name: Gautier Roussille
posts.cooked:
Howard, you’re welcome.
Of the top of my head, a few words about each producer:
Claire Naudin from Naudin-Ferrand has been making great * wines in the Hautes Cotes for quite some time. I believe I visited the estate for the first time in 2007. Her top cuvées are low/without sulfites and named for plants (viola odorata, bellis perenis, orchis mascula…). Her classic haute cote white and red are just on spot. If you like it, she as a beautiful range of aligoté especially some old vines. She also has some more prestigious terroir (echezeaux, aloxe) where she doesn’t take the same risks. Her reds are whole bunch, with the typical élégance of it (dry rose, pale color, etc.).

Bruno Lorenzon worked for a cooperage while consulting abroad (mainly South-Africa) before taking over the family estate. Based in Mercurey, not organic but very very dedicated viticulture. His winemaking is a little technical to me but flawless. His command of wood is especially impressive (he kept his connection to the cooperage to have hand-made barrels with specific wood and super light toasts). He use to make big extracted reds, now he’s at the opposite end of the spectrum. I especially like his whites (piece 15).

Soufrandiere, you might know their organic négoce “Bret brothers”. Two brothers in Pouilly-Fuissé, very very dedicated. Biodynamic. Great, simple, commited people. The whole range is top-notch, and their “aligoté aligato” hits my sweet spot.

Chateau des Rontets: Claire and Fabio Gazeau-Montrazi. One of the great terroir of Pouilly-Fuissé, a single block around the “Chateaux” (that for a variety of reasons won’t get the 1er cru classification). Lovely people, great organic wine. Maybe a little more classic in style.

  • I removed the term “organic” following William’s post

posts.name: J0zef P1nxten
posts.cooked:
Howard, you’re welcome.
Of the top of my head, a few words about each producer:
Claire Naudin from Naudin-Ferrand has been making great * wines in the Hautes Cotes for quite some time. I believe I visited the estate for the first time in 2007. Her top cuvées are low/without sulfites and named for plants (viola odorata, bellis perenis, orchis mascula…). Her classic haute cote white and red are just on spot. If you like it, she as a beautiful range of aligoté especially some old vines. She also has some more prestigious terroir (echezeaux, aloxe) where she doesn’t take the same risks. Her reds are whole bunch, with the typical élégance of it (dry rose, pale color, etc.).

Bruno Lorenzon worked for a cooperage while consulting abroad (mainly South-Africa) before taking over the family estate. Based in Mercurey, not organic but very very dedicated viticulture. His winemaking is a little technical to me but flawless. His command of wood is especially impressive (he kept his connection to the cooperage to have hand-made barrels with specific wood and super light toasts). He use to make big extracted reds, now he’s at the opposite end of the spectrum. I especially like his whites (piece 15).

Gautier, a couple of points/questions from my end:

  • Claire Naudin: I have had her “top cuvées are low/without sulfites” and those are definitely in the natural camp imho. So they don’t really fit the typical burg taste profile. Not my cup of tea but I think it’s good in general for reflexion on winemaking practices the cote d’or - evolution is always good.
  • Lorenzon: I just recently bought 6 bottles of the 2016 reds to try following some very positive comments of W. Kelley in an instagram video (worth a look, see below). I have access to some 2010s but haven’t bitten yet - around what period did his wines move more away from extraction? I am not against extraction if done well, but if the wines are no longer made in the same way, I will not tell me anything about the current style of the estate.

https://www.instagram.com/tv/B-ccF6xJfbq/

but I’m kind of disappointed to realize that the estate is not organic, when you’re almost there it’s just too bad.

I am interested in this comment. Is it necessarily bad to be almost there? I can remember one grower whose wines I like a good bit telling me he was almost there except that he disagreed that the use of copper was better than alternatives - I don’t remember the alternatives. In his case, I think his being almost there was a question of differences in what he believed was best for his vineyards and not in any desire for “efficiency” or cost reduction, etc., at least as he stated it to me. Can people be almost there for good reasons or do you believe that generally this is just making excuses. Interested in your thoughts. Thanks.

posts.name: Gautier Roussille
posts.cooked:
Jozef, the low sulfites wine from Claire can be amazing, but better taste before buying, some vintage I didn’t care for. About Lorenzon, I’m not exactly sure on the date of the shift but 2010 should be on the light style, 2011 definitely was (vintage helped but I don’t think Bruno shift from 0 to 1 in a single vintage). I have a 1990 I’m scared to open though. If you find some Piece 15 (white) 2012…

Howard, you’re organic or you’re not, being almost there only make sense in a transition period so if you’re there for a long time it’s not good for me (of course always better than full flag chemical…). One chemical/conventional (call it as you wish) treatment is enough to ruin the rest of the effort so I think “it’s too bad” not to just run the extra mile.
99% of winemakers who explain they are 99% organic are plain lying even though they not always realize it: “yeah, almost the same, just round-up once a year… sometime twice if needed. And one antifungal spray a year, a little more on wet years because I can’t afford to lose my harvest and three insecticides because we have issues withe grapes worms… but all the rest is organic”. How many time have I heard this speech. If the guy is really dishonest He’ll throw in a little “and we use some biodynamic practices”… Yes I have names, many, too many…

I appreciate the effort of many winemakers, Bruno included, who I never heard talking about organic (even now that William says he’s probably certified). They do what they think is good, they don’t need the world to applaud the effort. On the other hand I couldn’t smack hard enough on the cohort of winemakers who tell you all the good things they’re about to do… in a few years.

For Claire and some others, I have a felling that it’s a generation thing. New generations come in and go further and do better, and sometimes the elders (sorry Claire, you look good as always ) are stuck with practices that use to be the best around. They way they put it is really different too.

I turned berserk a few years ago when the CIVC (champagne) issued a publication explaining that all of Champagne was almost organic because more than 95% of the spraying was organic… they just decided to use weight of spraying product because it made them look good. Obviously visit champagne and look at the vine, you’ll see it’s just a big plain lie.

Copper toxicity at the dose used nowadays is bullshit (of course if you are organic just for the label and use 100% of authorized dose every year it doesn’t make sense either) used as an excuse but it’s been lied into so many’s mind… And everyone uses it (as shown by my Champagne anecdote), it’s the most used product even by conventional, sometimes they don’t even know they’re using it and at higher dose than organic.

Now I have a felling I’m trashing this thread so better move this discussion if you want to go further down that line.

    William Kelley on Instagram: "Burgundy’s powerful hierarchy of appellations has its winners and its losers. No region suffers more than the Côte Châlonnaise. But thankfully, producers such as Vincent Dureuil and Bruno Lorenzon are smashing glass ceilings to reveal the true potential of their terroirs."

My impression is that Bruno’s style in red has evolved gradually and incrementally rather than radically. He has known what he wanted to achieve in red wine from the beginning and has honed in on it over time. I wouldn’t hesitate to try a bottle or two. His 1999 Cuvée Carline is drinking great today, though obviously was more extracted when it was young (like so many producers two decades ago). But, I’m hoping to do a vertical with him, maybe later this year. I second what Gautier wrote about his intensity and focus, and would add that he drinks good wine—and not just from Burgundy. I think the fact that he has a cultivated palate is important. My impression is that many growers still drink mainly their own wines, or simple local wines, and if you have never tasted DRC or Leroy etc it limits your sense of the possible. Bruno knows all the reference points, and it shows.

Out of interest, I checked in on my Burgundy purchases for the last couple of years and found that there are three producers who I have clearly bought the most from over that period - Rossignol-Trapet, Dublere and Hudelot-Noellat.

Truchot

Dublere
Hudelot-Noellat
Bouchard
Chandon de Brialles
Ramonet
Jouan
Bernard Moreau

The biggest changes are the additions of Ramonet and Bernard Moreau to the top 10. It is because I am buying more from them, but also because I am now buying reds from them, as well as whites. In general, white Burgundy producers have not made my list in the past because I don’t hold whites as long as reds - premox issues. But, now there are four producers where I have a decent amount of both reds and whites on my top 10 list - Dublere, Bouchard, Ramonet and Moreau.

In terms of purchases in 2019 and 2020, the producers that I have purchased the most wines from, in order, are Bernard Moreau, Ramonet, Heitz-Lochardet, Hudelot-Noellat and then Clerget and Bouchard (all white).

We just started getting the Heitz-Lochardet wines locally. I have never tasted them, nor can I find much about the wines online. What is your experience with them if you wouldn’t mind sharing?

We just started getting the Heitz-Lochardet wines locally. I have never tasted them, nor can I find much about the wines online. What is your experience with them if you wouldn’t mind sharing?

I visited there in 2018, met Armand Heitz (who is very charming) and tasted a range of their wines. I liked the whites better than the reds, but both are quite good. I have also had a few of his villages level wines, which I loved (more importantly, my wife loved them). I have been buying a number of his wines (and some will be shipped here at the end of this month) but have not opened any of my premier crus yet.

Armand is a very young man (I think he is around 32) and this is becoming a much bigger domaine. The family has a number of hectares but have traditionally leased the land to Drouhin. https://www.pearlofburgundy.com/our-wines/domaine-heitz-lochardet/ He seems to be getting more and more back every year so as I understand it he now has a good bit more vines under his control than he did when I visited him. The domaine is still growing and I think this is becoming an important domaine. L'histoire d'Armand Heitz
